articulation: Jaw is fully open, tongue lies flat at the bottom of the mouth.
commonMistakes: Reducing it to a schwa in stressed positions.
teachingTips: Open your mouth wide like you are at the dentist.
Keep the tongue flat.

articulation: Mouth is slightly less open than for /a/, muscles are relaxed.
acousticDescription: Duller and shorter than the open /a/.
commonMistakes: Pronouncing it as a full open /a/ at the end of words.
teachingTips: Relax your jaw completely.
This is the sound of the 'a' at the end of almost every Portuguese word ending in 'a'.

minimalPairs.contrast: No contrast, allophonic in nasal context, but distinct in stress: par'a' vs p'a'ra

englishApprox: Similar to 'hey', but without the glide at the end
articulation: Tongue is raised high and forward, lips are spread but not tight.
commonMistakes: Adding a 'y' glide at the end (making it a diphthong like in 'day').
teachingTips: Smile slightly and hold the sound steady.
Do not move your jaw while saying it.
Think of the French 'é'.

articulation: Tongue is mid-height and forward, jaw drops lower than for /e/.
teachingTips: Drop your jaw more than you think you need to.
Keep the sound short and flat.

minimalPairs.contrast: /e/ vs /a/ (stress shift often changes vowel quality)

articulation: Tongue is very high and forward, almost touching the roof of the mouth.
commonMistakes: Making it too short like 'sit' (should be tenser).
teachingTips: Stretch your lips into a wide smile.
It's the 'ee' sound in 'cheese'.

articulation: Lips are rounded, tongue is back and mid-high.
commonMistakes: Adding a 'w' glide at the end.
teachingTips: Round your lips into a small 'o' shape.
Freeze your jaw; do not let it close as you finish the sound.

articulation: Lips are rounded but jaw is more open than for /o/.
teachingTips: Drop your jaw while keeping lips rounded.
Ideally, put a finger between your teeth to ensure openness.

articulation: Lips are very rounded and tight, tongue is high back.
commonMistakes: Relaxing lips too much (making it sound like 'book').
teachingTips: Make a 'kissing' face.
Push your lips forward.

englishApprox: No exact equivalent; try grunting 'huh' through your nose
articulation: Jaw relaxed, air flows through nose and mouth simultaneously. Tongue is low-mid.
commonMistakes: Pronouncing the 'n' or 'm' consonant fully (e.g., saying 'ban-da' instead of 'bã-da').
teachingTips: Touch your nose; you should feel vibration.
Do not let your tongue touch the roof of your mouth for the 'n'.

englishApprox: Like 'pain' but stop before the 'n' touches
articulation: Lips spread, tongue high-mid, air through nose.
teachingTips: Start to say 'aim' but pinch your nose shut halfway through.
Keep the tongue flat.

articulation: High front tongue position, air through nose.
commonMistakes: Pronouncing the 'm' or 'n' at the end.
teachingTips: Smile wide and buzz the sound in your nose.
It's like a mosquito buzzing 'iii'.

articulation: Rounded lips, back tongue, air through nose.
teachingTips: Round lips tightly.
Imagine the sound resonating in your forehead.

articulation: Very rounded lips, high back tongue, air through nose.
teachingTips: Make a fish face and grunt through your nose.

articulation: Lips close and burst open.
commonMistakes: Too much aspiration (puff of air) like in 'pin'.
teachingTips: Hold a piece of paper in front of your mouth; it should NOT move when you say 'pão'.

articulation: Lips close and burst open with vocal cord vibration.
teachingTips: Fully voice the sound from the throat.

englishApprox: Softer than English 't'; tongue touches teeth
articulation: Tip of tongue touches the BACK of the upper TEETH (dental), not the gum ridge.
commonMistakes: Touching the gum ridge (alveolar) like in English.
teachingTips: Touch your tongue to your teeth, not the roof of your mouth.
No puff of air.

articulation: Tip of tongue touches the BACK of the upper TEETH.
commonMistakes: Touching the gum ridge instead of teeth.
teachingTips: Push tongue against teeth.

articulation: Back of tongue touches soft palate.
teachingTips: Keep it dry and short.
No explosion of air.

articulation: Back of tongue touches soft palate, voiced.
teachingTips: Vibrate vocal cords.

articulation: Tongue blocks air then releases it with a hiss.
commonMistakes: Using it when not appropriate (only used for 't' before 'i' or 'e' in many accents, or spelled 'ch' which is actually /ʃ/). Note: Spelled 'ch' is SH sound. This phoneme is usually T before I.
teachingTips: In Brazil, when 't' is followed by an 'i' sound (like in 'leite'), it becomes 'chi'.
Think 'chee'.

articulation: Tongue blocks air then releases voiced hiss.
commonMistakes: Using /ʒ/ (like measure) instead of the affricate.
teachingTips: Whenever 'd' is followed by 'i' (or final 'e'), say 'j' like 'jeep'.
Cidade becomes 'Ci-da-jee'.

articulation: Top teeth touch bottom lip.
teachingTips: Same as English.

articulation: Top teeth touch bottom lip, voiced.
commonMistakes: Confusing with /b/ (common in Spanish but not Portuguese).
teachingTips: Keep the teeth on the lip and buzz.
Do not close your lips fully like a 'b'.

articulation: Tip of tongue near alveolar ridge, air hisses.
commonMistakes: Pronouncing 's' between vowels as /s/ (it usually becomes /z/).
teachingTips: Standard 's' sound.
Remember: 'S' between two vowels sounds like 'Z' (Casa = Caza).

articulation: Same as /s/ but voiced.
commonMistakes: Devoicing at the end of words (though rare in BP).
teachingTips: Buzz like a bee.
Use this sound when a single 's' is between vowels.

articulation: Blade of tongue rises towards hard palate.
commonMistakes: None phonetically, but orthographically 'x' can be tricky.
teachingTips: Round your lips slightly and hush.
In Portuguese, 'x' often makes this sound.

articulation: Same as /ʃ/ but voiced.
commonMistakes: Using /dʒ/ (hard J) instead. English speakers want to say 'Jungle' but it should be soft 'Zhungle'.
teachingTips: It is a soft sound, like a French 'J'.
Never touch your tongue to the roof of your mouth to stop the air.

articulation: Glottal or velar fricative.
commonMistakes: Pronouncing 'R' at the start of words like an English 'R'.
Rolling the 'R' (Spanish style) - usually too strong for BP.
teachingTips: If a word starts with 'R', pretend it is an 'H'.
Rio sounds like 'Hio'.
Double 'RR' is also an 'H'.

articulation: Lips closed, air through nose.
commonMistakes: At end of words, it indicates a nasal vowel, not a full 'm' sound.
teachingTips: Lips together.
If at the end of a word (sim), don't close your lips fully, just make the vowel nasal.

articulation: Tip of tongue on alveolar ridge, air through nose.
teachingTips: Standard 'n' sound.

articulation: Middle of tongue presses against hard palate.
teachingTips: Press the FLAT middle part of your tongue against the roof of your mouth.
It is one single sound, not two.
Think of the Spanish 'ñ'.

articulation: Tip of tongue touches alveolar ridge, air flows sides.
commonMistakes: Using the dark 'L' (from 'ball') at the start of words.
Note: At the END of words, L becomes 'W' (Brazil) -> 'Braziw'.
teachingTips: At the start of a word, use a light, forward 'L'.
At the end of a word, turn it into a 'U' or 'W' sound.

articulation: Middle of tongue presses against hard palate, air flows sides.
teachingTips: Touch the middle of your tongue to the roof of your mouth.
It's the liquid version of 'NH'.
Say 'million' but freeze on the 'lli' part.

articulation: Tongue quickly taps the alveolar ridge once.
commonMistakes: Using the 'H' sound (which is for double RR).
teachingTips: Don't roll it. Don't growl it.
Just a quick tap, exactly like American 'water' or 'better'.

articulation: Lips rounded, back tongue raised.
teachingTips: Often appears at the end of words ending in 'L' (Brazil = Braziw) or 'O' diphthongs (Pão = Paw-ng).

articulation: Front tongue raised high.
teachingTips: Standard 'y' sound.
Used in diphthongs like 'pai' (father).
